name: Anduril
overview: Anduril is a US defence-technology company, founded in 2017 by Palmer Luckey and others, that was built around AI and autonomy from the start rather than bolting them onto legacy hardware. Its core product is Lattice, a software platform that fuses sensor data and lets one person command many autonomous machines; on top of it Anduril builds drones, missiles, undersea vehicles and surveillance systems (Ghost, Roadrunner, Fury, Barracuda, Ghost Shark, Sentry towers). It has won major US, Australian and other allied contracts and partners with AI firms including OpenAI, Meta and Oracle.

established.label: Built for autonomy from day one
established.plain: Anduril was started in 2017 by Palmer Luckey (the Oculus VR founder) and four co-founders. Unlike traditional defence contractors, it was designed around software and AI first, then builds the hardware around it. By 2026 it had grown into one of the most valuable defence startups, valued in the tens of billions of dollars.

established.label: One operator, many machines
established.plain: Lattice is Anduril's central software platform. It pulls in data from many sensors, works out what it is seeing (detecting and tracking objects), and lets a single human operator direct many drones or other autonomous systems at once instead of one operator per machine. It runs on different hardware, not just Anduril's own, and sits at the heart of everything the company sells.

established.label: AI eyes on the US border
established.plain: Anduril built and deployed hundreds of solar-powered Sentry towers along the US southern border for US Customs and Border Protection. Each tower uses cameras, radar and AI to spot and identify movement automatically and alert agents, so people are not staring at screens all day. As of early 2025 these towers reportedly covered around 600 miles, roughly 30% of the border.

established.claim: Ghost Shark XL-AUV for the Royal Australian Navy
established.label: Large autonomous undersea drone
established.plain: Ghost Shark is a large uncrewed submarine designed for long-range surveillance, reconnaissance and strike missions without a human crew aboard. Anduril co-developed three prototypes with Australia's defence science group, delivering them on time and on budget by 2024. In September 2025 Australia signed a roughly A$1.7 billion (about US$1.1 billion) five-year contract for dozens of them.

established.label: A drone that hunts drones
established.plain: Roadrunner is a jet-powered drone that takes off and lands vertically. Its counter-drone version, Roadrunner-Munition, autonomously finds and can destroy incoming threats from small attack drones to larger aircraft. If the operator decides not to strike, it can fly back, land and be reused, which keeps the cost-per-shot low. A US customer was already buying it at launch.

inProgress.claim: Fury / YFQ-44A Collaborative Combat Aircraft for the US Air Force
inProgress.label: An AI 'wingman' jet
inProgress.plain: Anduril is one of two companies (with General Atomics) building the US Air Force's first Collaborative Combat Aircraft — semi-autonomous jets meant to fly alongside crewed fighters as robotic teammates. Anduril's aircraft is the YFQ-44A 'Fury'; it first flew in late 2025 and entered production at the company's Ohio factory in early 2026.

inProgress.claim: Taking over the Army's IVAS mixed-reality headset programme
inProgress.label: AI goggles for soldiers
inProgress.plain: Anduril took over from Microsoft the US Army's troubled programme to build mixed-reality combat headsets (IVAS), under a contract framework reported at up to $22 billion over ten years. The work continued into the Army's follow-on 'SBMC' competition, where Anduril is prototyping a headset called EagleEye that overlays sensor data and AI onto a soldier's view.

inProgress.claim: Lattice software for the Pentagon's Replicator drone initiative
inProgress.label: Software to fly drone swarms
inProgress.plain: The Pentagon's Defense Innovation Unit picked Anduril's Lattice software to coordinate large numbers of autonomous systems bought under 'Replicator', the US drive to field cheap, mass-produced drones quickly. The job is to let many uncrewed assets from different makers work together as a team under human supervision.

inProgress.claim: Meta partnership on AI/AR helmets for the US Army
inProgress.label: Tech-giant teaming on soldier headsets
inProgress.plain: In May 2025 Anduril and Meta announced they would jointly build AI-powered augmented-reality gear for soldiers, combining Meta's consumer VR/AR display technology with Anduril's Lattice software and defence systems. It is Meta's first major move into defence and feeds Anduril's EagleEye headset for the Army.

latest.label: ChatGPT-maker enters defence
latest.plain: In December 2024 Anduril and OpenAI announced a strategic partnership to apply OpenAI's AI models to counter-drone defence — helping systems detect, assess and respond to aerial threats faster and ease the load on human operators. It was OpenAI's most significant move into the defence sector and a reversal of its earlier ban on military uses.

latest.claim: First Ghost Shark delivered to the Australian Navy
latest.label: Autonomous sub fleet starts arriving
latest.plain: In November 2025 Anduril delivered the first production Ghost Shark extra-large autonomous undersea vehicle to the Royal Australian Navy, the opening hand-over under the multi-billion-dollar 2025 contract. It marks the programme moving from prototypes to real, fielded vehicles.

suppliers.name: US Department of Defense (Air Force, Army, SOCOM, DIU)
suppliers.role: Key customer — CCA jets, IVAS/SBMC headsets, Roadrunner, and Lattice for the Replicator drone programme
suppliers.claim: The transparency and teamwork between industry and government really accelerated how quickly we could mature the CCA program. — Air Force Sec. Frank Kendall on the Anduril/GA selection

suppliers.name: Australian Department of Defence / Royal Australian Navy
suppliers.role: Key customer — A$1.7bn (~US$1.1bn) Ghost Shark autonomous undersea vehicle contract
suppliers.claim: The Ghost Shark is the most high-tech long range autonomous underwater capability that exists in the world today. — Defence Industry Minister Pat Conroy
suppliers.sourceName: Anduril — Ghost Shark factory opens, Sydney

suppliers.name: US Customs and Border Protection
suppliers.role: Key customer — hundreds of Sentry autonomous AI surveillance towers on the southern border
suppliers.claim: These towers give agents in the field a significant leg up against the criminal networks that facilitate illegal cross-border activity. — Border Patrol Chief Rodney Scott

suppliers.name: OpenAI
suppliers.role: AI partner — large AI models for counter-drone (CUAS) defence on the Lattice platform
suppliers.claim: Our partnership with Anduril will help ensure OpenAI technology protects U.S. military personnel… to keep our citizens safe and free. — Sam Altman, OpenAI CEO

suppliers.name: Meta
suppliers.role: Technology partner — AI/AR display and optics for the EagleEye soldier headset (US Army)
suppliers.claim: We're proud to partner with Anduril to help bring these technologies to the American servicemembers that protect our interests at home and abroad. — Mark Zuckerberg, Meta CEO

suppliers.name: Oracle
suppliers.role: Cloud/compute partner — AI infrastructure from data centre to the tactical edge for Lattice
suppliers.claim: Anduril's Lattice on Oracle Cloud ties together those physical systems with the world's best cloud services, data management, and AI infrastructure. — Rand Waldron, VP, Oracle

sources.name: Breaking Defense — Australia Ghost Shark contract
Breaking Defense — Air Force CCA selection

unknowns: Many contract values are reported by press rather than confirmed line-by-line, and the IVAS/SBMC '$22 billion' figure is a ceiling for a programme still being recompeted, not a guaranteed award. The real-world performance, classified deployments and combat effectiveness of Anduril's autonomous systems are largely not public, and several partnerships (e.g. OpenAI) are described in broad terms without disclosed technical detail or dollar figures.
